name: Perioral dermatitis
dek: Small papules and pustules around the mouth, sometimes the nostrils and eyes. Often triggered by topical corticosteroids, fluoride toothpaste, or heavy occlusive products. The treatment is subtractive, not additive — the opposite of how most patients arrive.
oneliner: Stop everything. Then re-introduce, slowly. Often Rx.
bottom: The single most useful step is the one patients resist most: stopping every product that touched the area. The rash hates skincare; the treatment hates skincare too.

triggers.n: Spares a thin border at the lip margin. The 'clear zone' is the diagnostic giveaway.
Distinct from eczema — irritation is the dominant sensation, not itch.
Often paradoxical. Steroid initially helps, then drives the rash. The single most common iatrogenic cause.
Occlusion concentrates whatever flora the rash dislikes. Switch to lighter, fragrance-free formulations.

ingredients.name: STOP topical steroids
ingredients.evidence: Universal. Including hydrocortisone. The rash flares for 1–2 weeks then improves.

ingredients.name: Topical metronidazole 0.75%
ingredients.evidence: 8–12 weeks. The reference treatment in mild-moderate disease.

ingredients.name: Oral doxycycline 100 mg/d
ingredients.evidence: 8–12 week course. Effective; the workhorse oral. Standard dermatology prescription.

ingredients.name: Pimecrolimus 1%
ingredients.evidence: Useful when patient cannot tolerate metronidazole or wants steroid-sparing alternative.

ingredients.name: SLS-free, fluoride-free toothpaste
ingredients.evidence: Adjunctive trigger removal; small but consistent contribution to recurrence prevention.

phases.b: Stop topical steroids, fragrance, heavy moisturisers. Cleanser + lightweight ceramide moisturiser only.
Topical metronidazole 0.75% twice daily. Oral doxycycline if widespread or stubborn. 8-week minimum.
Most cases clear by week 12. If persistent, second-line oral or biologic referral. Recurrence is common; trigger discipline matters.
